speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Henry. If you would please tell us-- tell us about your-- the people you knew in your ice skating group-- especially your relationship with the Polish skaters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Especially with the-- with my Polish friends, I was skating with them. What's-- I mentioned before, I would-- I hate to repeat myself. It's the only thing where the-- I associated-- the-- they liked me, because they thought-- I don't know. I think so. They thought-- I act like they-- I'm-- the-- Polish people was different to say, the Jewish people-- different dress, different-- with always some of them with accent-- not all of them-- like myself. [LAUGHS] This is the reason they associate with me. They-- and-- and I enjoyed them, because they been-- good-- very good athletes. And I consider myself a good athlete in-- in the-- even now, I think-- I believe longevity if I'm-- already now 80-- 82 years. Because I've been, all my life a good athlete-- a good soccer player, a good bicycle, a good-- especially ice skating. I loved ice skating until today. And I even think someday I feel another-- because now I'm after serious surgery, I will feel better. I go back skating. I got fine skates. I paid a few hundred dollar. I couldn't afford it as something-- a kid in Poland. I couldn't afford it, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you were trying to get along with the Polish skaters and they put you down because you were a good Jew, and the rest of the Jews weren't so good?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the reason, because I tried to keep myself at a distance from them. I have give them a chance to see me different, because I had been a good athlete, and I'd been-- I-- we associated with the-- the Polish girls. We used to go on-- even I belonged to entire-- when I come home from there, I-- I-- I like I-- I-- I got two personalities in myself-- one personality way to Zionism, to this movement. I feel myself, like, very close. I-- most education, I took more-- a lot of reading in groups where there-- the more advanced in education. We advanced ourself. Or when I went-- I love because of athletic stature. I-- well, [LAUGHS] it's hard to say. I say, I've been good. I've been good. This, is the-- I think it's a big reason. And I went with them, I used to go bicycle, go out, and go places. [LAUGHS] But-- if-- my mother used to say, don't eat [INAUDIBLE]. Don't-- you know, in the home, we got a kosher kitchen, or a kosher-- and she know. And I didn't give the-- my Polish friends, I didn't give them a chance. I'm different from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that make you feel-- to have to be different?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I I don't know. I-- this was my nature. I-- I've been like this. And I talked with them. I've been very serious with them. I-- I-- I don't try to-- myself, I don't try to-- to be I'm difference. I-- I-- I'm a sincere person. Even they share with me-- they got their own-- went to school, to-- to school. The-- even-- used to-- we had done some special-- a friend of my brother, his girlfriend, I asked him-- I felt I need more education. I haven't got enough. It's-- I-- used even the Polish friends to-- to help me with Polish history, like things like that. I didn't give them a chance to think I'm different. This is the reason. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I never-- they--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They treat me like-- like I-- I am their owns. When I went to-- in the night-- special. I went back to-- to-- to the organization, to-- to-- to my group of people. When I-- I'm talking when I was a youngster. It was entire difference. I give lot of thought, but it's even now, so many years away, I give lot of thought. Because I-- that's what I've done. I've been sincere. I think that's why they accept me. And I learned from this. I had-- partners here in United States. I got to bring this up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's the same way I learned from this time. He-- he-- he used to tell me the same thing. Henry, you are different person than like I dealt with other. I didn't like it. He know I didn't like this saying, like I've been told this in Poland. He was a Canadian. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I live with him. I done some work with him in-- in a town here in-- in the United States, in Santa Maria. They build the Vandenberg Airbase. We done some work with him. I got a chance to live with him. He-- all together, until we build a office over there. I would like to drive every day-- every day 400 miles. Is-- we-- I live with him. He told me the same thing. And I learned it with my Polish friends over there. I considered them friends. They were doing everything during the war for Val. I got in contact with the-- they brought me some food. And even after the war, I got in touch-- after the war. We left right away. He-- he used-- he must be mine age, this-- must be around 35, 30. He worked in a-- in a big firm, a Polish firm. He brought me a lot of food, even after the war. I didn't ask him. I say, it's all right. That's what I learned during--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you learned to have one side of your personality show at one time, and another side show at another time. And you were successful at it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- my sister, this-- she used to say you, they didn't like it, what I was doing. I took the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't they like it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was-- the hate was so big. She-- when-- sister used to say, [NON-ENGLISH]. You're going to die between gentile-- between Polish people. Because so been-- big difference, in general. It was a small percentage like me, very small, but know how to handle. You've got to have a certain type of intelligence-- how to balance-- with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think-- do you think that this balance, this way of thinking that you developed as a boy in Poland, and as a young man, helped you to survive all the terrible things you're going to tell us about in a minute?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes-- helped me survive things like this. You was able to go along with people. You-- you-- you-- later on, during the war, I tried to-- to survive. You've been separated, like a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How to survive? You have to be in contact with outside people to bring some food-- doing the exchange during this time. You-- you had to go like this to be organized-- how to survive. That's what I learned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I couldn't say I was on by myself-- would lift-- this guy eventually was-- Radom was a city with 35,000 Jews. They had-- they had to make a living with-- with-- the Polish population, like you run a business. You run a-- the hate was surely-- the-- the-- is difference. Here you got the hate, let's say, on each other. Over there-- the government was like this. Like, the government, they made you make a sign. This is to recognize this is a Jewish institution, a Jewish store, a Jewish manufacturer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's say, if my name was Zilberschlag, I had-- could not the write Zilberschlag. Zilberschlag was more German. They make you write Hersch. Hersch was Jewish. They recognize-- the government done this. When did the government start doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: All my life. All my life it was doing, even with Jewish heroes in-- in-- in the-- in the Polish-- I don't know if I find words at this moment. I know the independence in Poland participate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ever heard [NON-ENGLISH]? A fame-- a Polish-- he give independence when-- I remember just this. My mother, my parents, they remember time when Poland was under the Germans, under the Russian, under-- list-- under the Russian. When -- what is the first incident you can remember of the war when you knew that the Nazis were coming? Tell me the first thing that you can think about that you remember. What happened to your family? And to you? And the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war. Well, we know the-- the stories-- what's-- what's going on before the war. He say a-- a big percentage for the Jewish population, like I said before, went to other countries, like special of the anti-Semitism education for the children. They went to Germany before '33. And Hitler come in '33 to power. They took the all of them. They called them [NON-ENGLISH] and sent back to Poland-- took them-- and send them back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: From them-- from them, we learn what is going on. They still-- [LAUGHS] --haven't been happy with the Polish. They've been ridiculous. Some of them even-- German Jews say to me, if-- [NON-ENGLISH], German that we could die from the Poles. The Poles was-- the first thing that they done to-- to-- to look us-- for Jews, they get a five pound sugar, or five pound salt, to show this is a [NON-ENGLISH]. Some Jews took off. And they went outside to try to organize some food or get in contact with some--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's go back a little bit. What was the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the start of the war. This was the start when the Pole-- when the German come. Maybe three months, four months later, they-- after a few months later, they put them in ghettos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when the Germans marched into Radom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you, and what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like, I try to remember. You say, if you learn when the [NON-ENGLISH] where the-- Hitler send them back to Poland. What is going on? We tried to run away. I took the bicycle. I ran away to the thousands-- hundreds. I ran away to the Russian-- to the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when was this, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- when the German came to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that even, in the 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] This must be the 9th or 10th month, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: September?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, before. [NON-ENGLISH] must be the October-- September-- October-- I don't remember. I have to figure out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you run away on a bicycle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was trying to cross the Russian zone, not to be with Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My whole family-- my sister-- mine-- my brother's wife went to-- to the-- border from the Russian-Polish border was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? I don't know what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me? I went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I-- sometime, maybe I could say an impulse. I got my parents. I got my friends. I got a lot of-- I left everything-- business. I accumulated lots of a lot of china and things like this. I say well, over there you don't know what is there. Over there is still-- you haven't saw the horrible things-- what they're doing. And I went back to Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were already-- you were already in the china business by this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I was already--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was already in the china business, yeah. I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I was in china business. I start with the parents. He start when he was 12. You had to-- 14, 15-- you have to work with the parents. Everybody--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in your 20s though, by this time, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. You went to the Polish border on your bicycle. How many days were you away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about three, four weeks away. It's-- I come back, I still tried--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage? Were you camping? How did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: During this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Three, four weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three, four weeks, I went-- mine-- brother-- where you say in the Polish army-- he was in the Polish army-- and his wife and child-- I-- when I went away the bicycle, I took my brother's wife and his child. She's alive. In the-- the-- her parents was living on the border within Russia-- Wlodawa-- inside been over there after-- been very wealthy, the rich Jews-- very wealthy, got lot of-- lumber mills, in-- storage and-- very rich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with them, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you in Russia by that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I haven't crossed the-- the border. I haven't-- I done a lot of thinking. I say, no. I'm not-- whatever is going to happen, I got to save whatever. You left already on the-- a lot of-- accumulated a lot of things. What you say here, you-- nothing. I don't know what is going to happen there. I never saw the Germans yet. As I-- during the-- the whole time I went in Germany, by the thousands-- 1,000 Germans. And I was not afraid. I was young, and I looked-- I was blond. [LAUGHS] And I looked like Gentile, and they wouldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now where did you see these Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you see the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I start to see the Germans when I start back home from Wlodawa from-- from the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any problems on the way back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no problems. They wouldn't recognize me. This is-- I am difference in a-- if they would recognize me I'm Jewish, I'd have to wear the band. So as soon as they came, every Jew have to show he's Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came back to Radom, what did you come back to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was the same thing I left. I left-- I got a few places of business. I got my own place of business is the before I left. That's what make me run away. They bombed the-- the city. I had a business near-- near a factory for ammunition. And the Germans bombed this place. And lot of people were killed, and this, that. But still, I came back. I tried to salvage lot of things what I left. During the war is a shortage, everything was-- became so expensive so-- and you tried to-- to make a living in this help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Germans already occupying the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what happened to your family then?

speaker: interviewee
text: They still been living in the same place, in the weeks, or maybe 1, 2, 3, or 4-- I don't remember exactly. I cannot give you a date. They start to concentrate-- to organize ghettos, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you-- to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I [LAUGHS] I went the same way where all of them Jews went-- to ghettos.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to a ghetto, where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Radom. The-- let's say you've got-- like I said before-- like 35,000 Jews. They give them-- let be 1/10 of the city, a part where the Jewish people used to live-- concentrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you with? Who were you with in the ghetto-- the first ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- with everybody-- with my parents, with my friends, with-- with-- with-- with the leadership. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like the hell-- where you got to learn to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe where you lived, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: You learn to live with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe your living conditions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's say if-- if my parents lived in-- let's say in a house, like a-- well, let's say, like, here, I would say a three bedroom house-- you-- you went-- they-- if they make a place like this, you-- you have to take it in people. Let's-- the 35,000 Jews lived-- let's say they give you a place where 1,000 Jews lived-- 10 in a-- in a-- in a room, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you live? You moved. Did you and your family move from the house you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a lot to make-- it's going to be a big story. You see, it was not so easy, it-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] People, they had money. I actually, like I said-- I came back. And I tried to salvage. I accumulated. You had some money in comparison with people what they haven't got money. It's-- for money, you could try to, say, to-- to-- get a better place to live. I had a place. We used to have-- let's say, a-- a-- job in place for in china, a job. We've been jobless. It's a wholesale. I had a-- a office over there. And I moved in the office. You pay money. The administration-- you know, the Germans, they haven't done the-- the dirty thing. The dirty thing, the Poles had done. It's for money, you could stay another month, another two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you arrange that? Who did you pay the money to? And what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: You find a time like this. You-- you find a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you? I. I-- tell me, I.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Let's say I got in touch with this department. There was Poles-- included the head in the department was a German. The administration was Poland-- and for money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find--

speaker: interviewee
text: You give him money, and-- and you-- you was able to stay longer, get a better place to live, until it was harder, harder, until--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- the first place you went to, after you left your own house--

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in my own little office. I lived, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you have to pay to-- how much of a bribe did you have to pay to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who will remember? You bribe not just with money. You give him a set of china. You got-- money was-- became right-- lots of-- [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, the Polish zloty was not-- start became less, less [INAUDIBLE] Was less, less-- is-- there was many different ways to do it. And you learn--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I just want to know what you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: You learn, you learn how to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived in your office.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest-- they went-- they went to a smaller place, let's say-- where the ghetto was. They was in a smaller place, let's say-- all of them in one room start-- before they got an apartment, in their own house. They moved in a small-- because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they stayed in their own building, but they moved to a smaller quarter.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- not their own building. The-- the Judenrat organized between self. And they-- and they-- tried to-- to-- to-- to assign many different places according how many people were in most--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in a time like this, is bribes. Even the Jews between self, they bribe. Because they want to live better. When want-- they got more money. They can buy more and whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the people in the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I know the name. And I-- and I had been very close. They organized [NON-ENGLISH]-- a police force of Yiddish, because-- if I know, I know. Yeah. Some of them was very good people. They've been-- they've been-- the time made you corrupted. The time made you became evil-- even like Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ever heard the word Kapo? Or policeman-- Jewish policemen? It's honest before. You didn't know the-- the police is going to be corrupted. It's going to be evil. They're going to cooperate with the German, and they're going to cooperate with the German [NON-ENGLISH]. Or they cooperate with the Polish police. Did you have a role to play? Were you in any of these?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- positively not-- positively not. My brother was for a little later one. They-- they changed the-- the-- you know, if you work with it, you have got to-- you had to be evil. You've got to-- if not-- they killed themselves. They was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, thank God. I haven't. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, OK, for how long a period of time did you live in this little office where you paid a bribe to do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until every time-- let's say there was 30,000 Jews. There was 30. They-- made a smaller ghetto for 18-- let's say I give you approximately, 18,000 Jews. And later-- later until it comes to a point was from the 30,000 was left 2,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening to all those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were sent to death camps. They went Treblinka--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know? At that time, did you know? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- most of the people didn't believe this is possible they're going to do with them what they're doing. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you believe?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, I-- we didn't believe it's going to be so bad-- they going to kill you. They're going to-- little by little, you start to believe this-- everything. Because in these camps, it was brave kids that ran away that-- that told the people, take a look, whatever-- to organize a [NON-ENGLISH]-- to try to organize themself or to make a revolution, or-- it was impossible to do. And like the-- they took away the-- the-- they new the leadership. They took-- before they took the-- the-- the intelligentsia, let's say the profession, they took away the doctors. They done this-- this way--
